See the screens
Product design case study · the third Second Brain piece · 2026

How I turned raw usage data into signals a CTO can read, trust, and act on in one morning.

Every status meeting, every audit scramble, every "who actually knows this system?" panic: the answers already exist inside the team's AI sessions. The leadership layer pulls them upward as patterns, each one labeled with how confident we are, keeping the raw activity separate from the judgment calls. And a second problem showed up on its own: enterprises funding AI-driven development at scale could see token usage everywhere, but couldn't see what that spend actually bought, anywhere.

My role · Product Designer Status · design-stage, on live captured data Companion to the Second Brain & Session Viewer studies
Start with the two doors Jump to the screens
second-brain · leadership · monday 9:00 am
STALLSpark has been quiet 3 days, blocker never escalatedact
RISKRelay: 85% of its knowledge sits with one engineerplan
SPENDToken spend +18% MoM. 71% now mapped to shipped outcomesread
TALENTA junior engineer's sessions look senior, hidden expert flaggedmeet
No meeting produced this, it came straight from the work.
Overview & impactThe 60-second version

What this is, in a paragraph.

The first two case studies gave the developer a mirror and made autonomous AI work auditable. This one climbs the org chart. Two market doors opened at once: leaders running engineering on self-reported status, meetings extracting information the sessions already hold, and organizations buying compute at scale with no map from spend to value, an industry-wide enterprise challenge by 2026. Second Brain's leadership layer answers both from the same captured data, filtered so that detail stays with the developer and only patterns travel upward. It is the youngest layer of the product: designed on live capture from 100+ developers, not yet in a CTO's hands, and this page says so plainly.

Problem

Engineering leadership runs on substitutes: scheduled syncs and filtered self-reported updates, always late, always polished, while the honest record sits unread inside the team's AI sessions. In parallel, token spend became a first-class budget line with no map to outcomes; usage was visible everywhere, and usage is exactly the number that gets gamed.

Solution

A leadership layer over the same captured work: signals composed from many sessions, each carrying its confidence; twelve leadership intelligences in one command center; token economics with a "what it bought" column so spend never detaches from outcomes; and a decision registry that writes itself. Detail stays with the developer, only patterns travel up.

My role

Product Designer: the two-door framing, the trust boundary, and the three confidence states are mine, designed as the bridge between engineering realities and what a leadership reader can responsibly act on.

Impact
  • Status theater: roughly 8 hours of weekly syncs, six of them pure status extraction, replaced by ~1.5 hours of decision-only conversations (illustrative scenario, real mechanism).
  • The substrate is real: capture is shipped and in internal rollout, 100+ developers generating the exact data these surfaces read.
  • One claim already proven: the registry mechanism has already run on this project's own history.
The complex problemTwo doors, one root cause

The work already has the answers. Leadership just never hears them.

Both doors open onto the same root cause: the richest record of what an engineering org knows, decides, and struggles with, its AI sessions, is invisible above the individual developer. So leadership runs on substitutes.

Door 1 · Status theater

A leader's week is built around extracting information people already have: scheduled syncs, filtered self-reported updates, follow-up clarifications, emergency catch-ups. The substitute record is always late and always polished. Meanwhile the honest record, who is stuck, what got reworked, which decision was actually made and why, sits in the sessions, unread. The cost is concrete: a leader spending 8 hours a week in PM syncs, roughly six of them pure status extraction, getting a picture that is still days behind the ground truth.

Door 2 · The token reckoning

The second door I can quote in the exact words that opened it, typos preserved:

the observation that started this piece · 2026 · verbatim

"every enterprise is buying tokens for their developers as part of ai driven development, but the biggest issue is there is no visibility where those tokens are getting utilised"

The industry then proved the point at scale, though there's an important correction worth making honestly here. Enterprises could see usage; that was the problem. Through 2026 this hardened into a challenge every large engineering organization is now naming: some of the largest engineering organizations dismantled internal token-usage leaderboards after usage became the target and got gamed, and annual AI budgets were reportedly exhausted in months with no clear link between the spend and shipped output. Visibility of motion existed everywhere. What no one had was a map from motion to value, what the tokens actually bought: decisions made, problems solved, work shipped.

The claim, stated carefully: usage dashboards just measure activity. Second Brain measures the judgment behind that activity, the decisions and problem-solving it actually represents. The metric the industry publicly shifted toward, work that actually ships, is exactly what this product's outcome layer tracks. This isn't some new metric we invented; it's the same one the largest engineering orgs landed on after their usage dashboards stopped working. As organizations rely more on AI agents, that link between spend and results stops being a nice-to-have report and becomes the actual budget conversation.

Six things a leader cannot see today

Each of these is answered by a surface in the execution section. The examples are illustrative; the mechanisms are real.

01

Status is self-reported.

Four PM syncs a week produce filtered summaries, and it still takes days to feel ground truth. The sessions know the truth the moment it happens.

02

Rework is invisible.

Ticket boards show tickets completed, not that four requirement changes quietly cost 12 developer-days of net reversal. The PM feels something is slow; nobody can say what.

03

Stalls surface as missed deadlines.

A project can sit stuck for 18 days with the blocker never escalated, nobody flagged it because nobody saw it. Session activity shows the stall by day 3.

04

Bus factor is a guess.

Knowledge maps are built from self-reported skills. The behavioral record shows one engineer holding 85% of a critical service's working knowledge, visible before they resign, not after.

05

Decisions evaporate, and talent hides.

Architecture calls made in sessions are never documented; meeting dynamics bury junior contributions. A junior engineer's sessions can look senior, and no system notices.

06

Token spend has no value map.

The one AI number leadership gets, usage, is the one that gets gamed. Nothing connects the spend to decisions captured, problems solved, or work shipped.

The constraints I designed within

Every decision in the sections that follow traces back to one of these three groups. They are the design material of this piece, not its fine print.

Technical
  • Signals are composed from noisy evidence. No ground-truth labels exist for "risk" or "stall"; patterns are inferred from raw machine logs of real work, so every claim must carry its confidence and its evidence trail.
  • Correctness over freshness. A lesson inherited from the developer mirror: signals land composed and consistent rather than instant, because a wrong-day number costs more trust than a late one.
  • The substrate is local-first capture, translated once into readable events; the leadership layer reads what the shipped capture pipeline produces, never the raw transcripts.
Business
  • Compute spend must map to outcomes. The token view exists to answer "what did the spend buy?", and it must never degrade into the usage leaderboard the industry already dismantled.
  • Sequencing was a product constraint. The leadership layer had to wait for the capture substrate to be real; I cut it from scope twice rather than design something that just looked confident without being true.
  • One designer crossing into engineering, carrying the concept, surfaces, and clickable console within the same timeline as the other two pieces.
Ethical
  • Developers are the protected party. A leadership layer over developer sessions is one bad design decision away from surveillance; the surveillance fear is a first-class design input.
  • Aggregates only, structurally. Above the individual, drill-down to a person's session does not exist as a capability, not as a permission that could be toggled.
  • Subject-first disclosure. A named flag reaches its subject first, with a contest window, before any leadership surface.
Research & empathyThe working record is the user research

Three readers, one protected party.

This piece has one primary source: the project's real working record. The token observation in the problem section is quoted verbatim from it, typos preserved; it is this project's real user research, the actual users and the actual moment of need, not a persona invented after the fact. From that record and the console's own segmentation, three archetypes shaped every screen.

Reader one
The Hands-On Director

Wants: to open every drill-in, trace a flagged risk back to its composing signals, and judge the inference themselves before acting on it.

Fears: a dashboard of fiction, confident charts over data nobody can interrogate.

So the design must: make every claim openable to its evidence trail, keep signal composition inspectable, and never round an inference up to a fact.

Reader two
The Board-Facing CTO

Wants: the one-line truth: org health, knowledge risk, and spend mapped to outcomes, readable at board pace in one morning.

Fears: acting on a phantom risk. One false alarm acted on and the digest is dead to them.

So the design must: speak in declaratives only at high confidence, ask questions when unsure, and stay silent below threshold. It must never cry wolf.

The protected party
The Observed Developer

Wants: their detail to stay theirs. The raw record is their private mirror, not leadership material.

Fears: a leaderboard disguised as a dashboard; being ranked by a number derived from their private work.

So the design owes them: no drill-down above the individual, no per-person value metric anywhere in the product, and subject-first disclosure with a contest window before any named flag travels.

The two leadership archetypes are the same person on different days: the console's CTO persona is deliberately two readers in one, and every surface has to serve both without bending the trust rules. The third archetype never reads this console at all, which is exactly why they are on this list: the design's hardest requirements come from the person who isn't in the room.

The questions a leader can finally ask the work itself

"What did our token spend actually buy this month?"
TodayA usage chart. Who used the most tokens, the number the industry learned gets gamed the moment it becomes a target.
With Second BrainSpend mapped to outcomes: decisions captured, problems solved, sessions that shipped, and the unmapped share flagged for enablement, not punishment.
"If one person left tomorrow, what would break?"
TodayA manual audit, days of work, built on who claims to know what, usually wrong.
With Second BrainA behavioral knowledge map, continuously updated: one engineer holds 85% of a critical service, visible while there's still time to spread it.
"What did we decide three weeks ago, and why?"
TodayMeeting decisions and implementation decisions live in systems that never meet. Nobody can reconstruct the why.
With Second BrainA decision registry that writes itself from sessions and meeting cross-references, every entry linked to the moment it was made.
"Who is actually good at this, not by job title?"
TodaySeniority bias and agenda pressure bury junior contributions; hidden capability is invisible by definition.
With Second BrainHidden experts surface from behavior, the junior engineer whose sessions look senior gets flagged for the opportunity, not overlooked.
Ideation & IAThe system before the paint

Detail stays with the developer. Only patterns move up to leadership.

The leadership layer is an information architecture before it is a set of screens: private mirrors at the bottom, one aggregate view at the top, and a boundary between them that is drawn as a wall because it behaves like one. These artifacts are the skeleton the console was built on.

The system map · from private mirrors to one unified view

Level 0 · privateDeveloper mirrors

Each developer's own sessions, transcripts, and daily mirror. Full detail, evidence links, correction rights. The raw record never leaves this level.

detail stops here
Level 1 · composedComposed signals

Patterns composed across sessions and developers: rework, stalls, workarounds. Each signal carries its confidence and an openable evidence trail. No transcripts cross the wall.

→
Level 2 · the consoleTwelve intelligences

The command center's vocabulary: vital signs, attention items, and twelve leadership intelligences in health tiers, each ranking by confidence, never by noise.

→
Level 3 · the orgOne unified view

Compute and outcomes on one surface: token economics with its "what it bought" column, knowledge risk, and the decision registry, for the whole org.

The boundary is part of the design, not a policy bolted on top. Above the individual level, only combined patterns exist, so the layer is private by structure, not by a rule someone could switch off. That same choice is also what makes it scale: because only combined patterns travel upward, the org view stays one unified view of spend and outcomes whether it covers one team or fifty.

Developer
Everything, privately

Their own sessions, transcripts, and mirror. Transcripts stay here.

PM / team lead
Project signals

Composed patterns, rework, stalls, risks, per project. No transcripts, no per-person surveillance.

CTO / board
Strategic aggregates

Org-level health, spend-to-value, knowledge risk. No drill-down exists at this level.

⚠
One tension we're being upfront about: a named flag is still individual detail “One engineer holds 85% of Relay” and “a junior’s sessions look senior” are judgments about named people, derived from private work, arguably more sensitive than any transcript. The design answer is subject-first disclosure: an individual flag appears in that developer’s own mirror first, with the evidence and a contest window, before it can reach any leadership surface. Nothing about a named person travels upward unseen or uncontested by them. This is a deliberate design choice in the console shown here, and it's the one rule we think really matters if consent is going to hold up once this is actually in use.

The user flow · one signal's journey up the hierarchy

The founding scenario of this piece, first as the narrative it was pitched as, then as the flow the console actually implements. Watch what travels at each level, and what deliberately doesn't.

Tuesday8:14 am
A developer works around a payments constraint in a session: a workaround, encoded in plain language, captured automatically. Nobody writes a report. Nobody needs to.
Tuesday9:02 am
A second session on the same project hits rework, a requirement change reversing yesterday's work. Then a third: a scope change at 9:34. Three signals, three different developers, one project.
Tuesday9:40 am
The signals compose. Individually each says little. Together, workaround + rework + scope change on the same payments project, they form a risk pattern worth roughly 2.8 developer-days, carried upward with its confidence attached: high, three independent signals from three sessions, every one openable. The PM sees the pattern; they do not see anyone's transcript.
Wednesday9:00 am
The CTO's digest carries the composed risk with its evidence trail. The conversation that follows starts at the decision, descope or re-staff, not at "so, what's the status of payments?" The meeting that would have discovered this next sprint never needs to happen.
Tue8:14 am
Session signal captured. A payments workaround is encoded in plain language inside a normal working session. Identity-scoped detail stays in the developer's private mirror.
↓ two more sessions land on the same project
Tue9:40 am
Signals compose. Workaround + rework + scope change, composed into one candidate risk pattern.
↓ the pattern meets the gate every signal must pass
The gate
Confidence threshold. The composed pattern is scored on independence and coverage. Only what clears the bar may speak in declaratives upstairs.
Clears · High confidence. Ranked into the PM view and the CTO digest as a finding, ~2.8 developer-days at risk, evidence trail attached, next action proposed.
Doesn't clear · held. Logged, not surfaced; re-evaluated as new sessions land.
↓ only the cleared branch travels
Wed9:00 am
The CTO acts. One morning, telemetry to judgment: the digest opens where the conversation should start.

UI autopsy · the command center, on the real screen

The deconstruction is done on the console itself, a high-fidelity mockup recreated from the live production UI with dummy data: five structural decisions, each marked where it lives on the screen. The genuine earlier iterations live in the testing section below.

12345
1

The five vital signs sit at the very top, before you even have to scroll. Five org-level numbers with trends sit at the top: audit readiness, mandate adoption, culture, stuck density, token growth. They're ordered by how urgently you'd need to act on each one.

2

Attention ranks by confidence, not recency. "What needs your attention" is a ranked list; a high-confidence pattern outranks a softer, newer one, so the top of the list is always the safest thing to act on.

3

The whole org's health, summed up in one line. Seven healthy, three watch, two critical: the twelve intelligences roll up into health tiers, so a leader can scan a handful of tiers instead of twelve separate tiles, and only the ones that have slipped need a second look.

4

Every item shows its own status clearly. Each attention card carries its decision context (strategic, bid or no-bid, founder) on the surface, so the reader never has to guess how the system is reading the signal.

5

What's deliberately left off this screen matters just as much as what's on it. Every item names a department or a pattern, never a person to click into. No person-level drill-down exists on this surface, and below-threshold signals never render. Those omissions are doing real work, they're not an oversight.

Prototyping & testingThe iterations, then the console under stress

Three iterations to get to the console, then two scenarios built to stress-test it.

Every leadership dashboard fails the same way: it looks great in a demo, because demo data is always clean and well-behaved. This section shows the real iteration history from the working record, then tests the design against two messy scenarios on purpose, a team way over budget and an org nearly ten times the size of the demo, because a console that only works for the demo isn't actually usable day to day.

The iterations, and what each one fixed

These are real earlier prototypes from the working record, all using mock data. The first version proved the idea could win people over, but that's all it did, it wasn't actually usable yet. The second version made it operable, and that's when it became clear what was still missing. The third version added the pieces the whole story had been building toward.

Iteration 1 · the concept, as a storyA scroll-through pitch walking through one signal's journey upward. It was persuasive, but none of it was actually clickable or usable. What it needed next: stop narrating the idea and let a leader actually run their morning through it.▶ open this iteration
Iteration 2 · console v1The first working command center: vital signs, attention items, intelligence tiers. Most of this structure carried through to the final version; what it was still missing was the economics view and the registry, the two screens that answer the token-spend problem and the "what did we decide?" question.▶ open this iteration
Final · the consoleThe console, further along: the same command center, plus the two views this whole case study is about, token economics and the decision registry, with the confidence states now built in as real components instead of just labels.▶ open this iteration

Stress state A · a team at 240% of monthly budget

The token economics screen exists to keep spend attached to outcomes. The month that tests that promise is the ugly one: a team lands at nearly two and a half times its budget. No screenshot exists for this state, so it is rebuilt here in the design system exactly as specified: a designed edge state with illustrative data from the same dummy universe as the console.

second-brain · leadership · token economics · stress statedesigned edge state · illustrative data
TeamBudget usedWhat it boughtSignal
Payments platformpinned to top9 engineers · acme.dev
240% of monthly budget
9 decisions captured6 problems solved2 releases shipped41% of spend unmapped
act
High confidenceThe overrun traces to three rework loops on the payments dashboard, the same composed pattern flagged Tuesday. Proposed next step: settle the requirement churn before the next sprint. 3 signals · 3 sessions · evidence trail attached.
Relay11 engineers
84% of monthly budget
14 decisions captured11 problems solved4 releases shipped
read
Spark7 engineers
61% of monthly budget
8 decisions captured5 problems solved3 releases shipped
read
What the UI does when the numbers look bad. The over-budget row pins to the top and gets the alert styling, but the "what it bought" column stays right next to it, so spend is never shown without the outcomes it produced, even in the worst month. And instead of just a bare alarm, a high-confidence signal proposes what to do next. It's easy for a spend view to turn into a blame view when a team goes over budget; keeping outcomes and a next step attached is what keeps this a budget conversation instead of a finger-pointing exercise.

Stress state B · fifty teams instead of six

The console's demo org runs a handful of teams; an enterprise division runs fifty. Dashboards that look great with five rows stop working at fifty. Same honest label: designed edge state, illustrative data, no screenshot pretended.

second-brain · leadership · token economics · 50 teamsdesigned edge state · illustrative data
⌕  Filter 50 teams by name, budget state, or signalOver budget · 3Drifting · 5Steady · 42
Needs attention3 teams
Payments platform9 engineers
240% of monthly budget
2 releases shipped41% unmapped
act
Checkout6 engineers
131% of monthly budget
3 releases shipped22% unmapped
plan
+ 1 more over budget
Drifting5 teams
Relay11 engineers
84% of monthly budget
trend +9% MoM
plan
+ 4 more drifting
Steady42 teams
42 teams within budget, rolled into one line · pooled spend at 61% of pooled budget expand to list
How this handles fifty teams instead of six. A flat list of fifty teams doesn't work, so teams get grouped by how much attention they need: the teams that need action rise to the top, everything steady gets rolled into one summary row, and search and filters only show up once there are enough teams to justify them. This works at any scale because teams are ranked by how much attention they need, not by how many of them there are.
Final executionThe leadership console, every frame links to a clickable mockup

Real screens, not just a promise.

A high-fidelity, fully clickable leadership console with dummy data, a CTO persona over 127 engineers and 6 teams. Navigation, views, and detail pages all work; the numbers are illustrative and consistent. This is the design-stage layer, shown as exactly that, and every screen below names the constraint from the problem section that forced it into its shape.

Screen 1 · Command center. "what needs me today?"
second-brain · leadership · command center▶ open the clickable mockup
Command center. Five vital signs with trends, "what needs your attention" ranked, and twelve leadership intelligences in health tiers. This screen exists because of the ethical constraint: aggregates only, no person-level drill-down anywhere; and the technical one: signals are composed from noisy evidence, so every number links back to its evidence and nothing is shown without backup, a rule carried over from the developer mirror.
Screen 2 · Token economics. "motion vs. judgment"
second-brain · leadership · token economics▶ open the clickable mockup
Token economics, the second door, answered. Spend rolled up per team with a "what it bought" column: decisions captured, problems solved, sessions that shipped. The unmapped share is flagged for review, framed as an enablement question, never a leaderboard. Low usage reads as a coaching need, not a ranking. This screen exists because of the business constraint: compute spend must map to outcomes, or the view degrades into the usage leaderboard the industry already dismantled.
⚠
The trap this screen must not rebuild A “% of spend mapped to value” number can turn into the next gamed leaderboard, the exact problem this page is trying to avoid. Three safeguards are built into the structure, not just written as labels: teams are shown against their own trend, never ranked side-by-side by default; no per-person value metric exists at any level of the product; and there is deliberately no way to export mapped% into goal-tracking tools. The residual tension is real and named: any number a leader watches changes behavior. The honest defense is that outcomes (decisions kept, work shipped) are far harder to fake than token counts, gaming this metric mostly means doing the job.
Screen 3 · Domain & knowledge. "what breaks if…"
second-brain · leadership · domain & knowledge▶ open the clickable mockup
Domain & knowledge. Behavioral bus-factor: concentration risks flagged from what people actually do, and hidden experts get surfaced, like the cybersecurity depth that turned out to be sitting inside the DevOps team without anyone noticing. Pain 04 and the hidden-talent half of 05, answered structurally. This screen exists because of the ethical constraint: its flags are judgments about named people, so every one of them is governed by subject-first disclosure, the developer sees it in their own mirror, with a contest window, before leadership does.
Screen 4 · Decision registry. "what did we decide, and why?"
second-brain · leadership · decision registry▶ open the clickable mockup
The decision registry, writing itself. Decisions recovered from sessions and meeting cross-references, each with its source and status. This screen exists because of the technical constraint: signals composed from noisy evidence need proof, and this is the one leadership claim the earlier case studies could already substantiate, the same mechanism that recovered this project's own decisions. It also answers the decisions half of pain 05.

The component system · three confidence states

Composition is an inference, and inferences are sometimes wrong. At this level, a false positive isn't just a minor UX issue: if a CTO deprioritizes something because of a risk that turns out to be fake, they'll stop trusting the digest for good. So confidence is treated as a first-class part of the console's UI kit, three states, each with its own typography, iconography, color, position, and rules about what actions it's allowed to trigger. If the digest cries wolf, people learn to tune it out; this hierarchy is how the console guides attention without ever forcing someone to act.

●High confidence
●Payments absorbed 3 reversals this week. ~2.8 dev-days. 3 signals · 3 sessions.
open the evidence trailproposed: settle the requirement churn
TypographyA declarative sentence in full-weight ink. It states a finding and names its cost, because it can prove both.
IconographyA solid marker. The only state that earns a filled shape.
ColorStatus color on the chip and marker only; the message stays in ink. Color marks rank, not urgency theater.
LayoutRanked at the top of the digest, above every other state, ordered by confidence then cost.
ActionTwo affordances: open the evidence trail, and a proposed next step. The only state allowed to propose an action, because it is the only one that can back it.
◐Unsure
◐Possible risk forming on payments. 2 of 3 signals present. Worth a look?
view the partial signals
TypographyA question, never a claim. Regular weight, muted lead-in; the sentence form itself carries the uncertainty.
IconographyA half-filled marker: the pattern is literally incomplete, and the icon says so.
ColorAmber on the chip, nothing else. No alert treatment anywhere near an unproven pattern.
LayoutAlways below every High item regardless of recency; the slot in the stack communicates the state before a word is read.
ActionOne quiet affordance: view the partial signals. Nothing is proposed. The leader is invited to look, never pushed to act.
○Below threshold
○1 weak signal · held · re-evaluated as new sessions land
held-signals log
TypographyNone. Below threshold does not render a sentence on the digest; absence is the treatment.
IconographyNone on the digest. A count in the held-signals log is all that remains visible.
ColorNone. Muted mono in the log, indistinguishable from housekeeping, because that is what it is.
LayoutNot on the digest at all. It lives in a log the leader can open, and almost never needs to.
ActionNone offered. Held signals re-evaluate automatically as new sessions land; silence is a feature until evidence composes.

The hierarchy does most of the work here: attention goes to the top card first because everything below it is visually quieter, and the digest never sounds a false alarm, because only the state that can back up its claim with evidence is allowed to suggest an action.

RetrospectiveEach reflection anchored to a documented decision in this project's record

What building the third layer taught me.

What I learned about ethical design

1
Privacy is what makes the leadership layer work, not a limitation on itAt first the no-drill-down rule looked like it would limit how useful this could be for leadership. It's actually the reason the whole thing works. Developers agree to the capture because they know their detail can't travel upward, and that's exactly why leaders end up with truth they can rely on: because developers trusted the system enough to let it happen. The trust rules aren't overhead added on top of the product, they're a core part of what the product actually is.
2
The leaderboard problem has to be solved in the design, not with a policy memoAny number a leader keeps watching ends up changing people's behavior; the industry proved that at scale when the big usage leaderboards got gamed and had to be shut down. That's why the token view has three safeguards built into its structure. It's also why subject-first disclosure matters so much: a flag about someone always reaches that person before it reaches anyone above them. You can tell people to follow a policy and they might not, but you can't route around a rule that's built into the structure.

What I learned about system architecture

3
I cut the leadership layer from the product twice, and that was the right callBuilding leadership screens before the capture system actually existed would have meant confident-looking charts sitting on top of data we didn't actually have. The order mattered because it was really a trust decision: prove the capture worked with developers first, earn their consent second, and only then build the layer that depends on both being real. A leadership console is usually the first thing a buyer asks to see, which is exactly why it should be the last thing you actually build.
4
The market caught a flaw in my framing before any reviewer couldMy first version of the token pitch, "no visibility where tokens go", was subtly wrong: the biggest orgs already had that visibility, and that visibility is exactly what got gamed. The more honest claim is narrower and stronger: people could always see how tokens were being used, they just couldn't see what value came out of it. Getting that distinction right is the difference between a pitch that a sharp CTO takes seriously and one they pick apart in the first five minutes.
5
Confidence needed to be a real part of the UI, not just a data field behind the scenesThe digest is built around three states, and only one of them is allowed to state something as fact. Once confidence decides the typography, the icon, the position, and whether something is even allowed to suggest an action, the layout itself is doing a lot of the reader's thinking for them: they can trust what's at the top of the list because the page has already worked out how much doubt to attach to everything on it.

The honest limits, kept in full

One part of this already runs. The decision registry is not illustrative, it is the same mechanism that recovered this project's own decisions from session history. The registry screen shows the productized version of something that has already worked. That's the one claim here that makes the rest of this credible.
The substrate is real. Capture has been in internal rollout long enough that these surfaces read live data, not a fixture; the leadership layer is a projection of a feed that already exists at scale.
The market validated the pain while this was being designed. The industry's 2026 token reckoning is the strongest external evidence in any of these three case studies: the largest engineering orgs dismantled motion-metrics and pivoted toward exactly the outcome layer shown here, an industry-wide correction this design anticipated.
The rest is design-stage, and labeled. No CTO runs this console yet. The composed-signal examples (the 2.8 dev-days, the 18-day stall) are illustrative scenarios built on real mechanisms, not measured deployments. The stress states in the testing section are designed edge states, shown as exactly that.
The metrics this layer will be judged by, named up front: status-meeting hours replaced by digest reads; share of token spend mapped to shipped outcomes; knowledge-concentration risks flagged before a departure rather than after. Until a real leadership team actually uses it, every claim above is a prediction, not a result, the same discipline carried over from the first two studies.
✎
What was mine I architected the interaction models and defined the system constraints: the two-door framing, the trust boundary, the confidence states, and every surface of the clickable console. The execution ran inside guardrails I designed, one person carrying design into build, with the composition mechanisms being built with our engineering team on the shipped capture layer. Every decision here was a bridge between an engineering reality, noisy evidence, real budgets, real logs, and what a leadership reader can responsibly act on.
In closingThe trilogy, complete

One system that captures the work, shown at three different altitudes.

The developer gets a private mirror of their own work. Whoever is building autonomous agents gets a way to see what those agents actually did. Leadership gets the combined picture, the real state of things, plus a clear line from AI spend to what it actually produced. Three case studies, one point: the useful intelligence was always sitting inside the everyday work; the real design problem was building something that could surface it without breaking anyone's trust.

A note on the evidence The opening observation is quoted verbatim from this project's record, typos preserved. The 2026 industry events are paraphrased from public reporting and presented as an industry-wide enterprise reckoning, no single company singled out. Scenario examples are labeled illustrative; the screens are a clickable design-stage console with dummy data; the two stress states are designed edge states built for this study, never passed off as screenshots. The shipped substrate and its scale are stated per the same three-layer honesty as the companion studies.
SBSecond Brain · Leadership · a case study

The same captured work, surfaced as the answers leadership pays for, status without meetings, knowledge risk before departures, and token spend finally mapped to value.
